metadata.working_title: Benchmarking computational methods for inferring copy number aberrations from single-cell RNA-seq using paired single-cell multi-omics

abstract_sketch.problem: Copy number aberrations (CNAs) drive cancer initiation and progression, and several tools now infer them directly from scRNA-seq data, but an independent head-to-head evaluation against ground-truth scDNA-seq has been lacking.
abstract_sketch.gap: No prior benchmark has used paired single-cell multi-omics (scDNA-seq + scRNA-seq in the same cell) to evaluate tumor-normal classification, CNA profile accuracy, subclonal structure inference, and aneuploidy detection in non-malignant cells across the five most widely used methods.
abstract_sketch.approach: We evaluated inferCNV, CopyKAT, SCEVAN, Numbat, and CaSpER on eight colorectal cancer samples, two acute lymphoblastic leukemia samples, one glioma, one neuroendocrine tumor, an NPC43 cell line, HUVEC normal controls, and a gastric sample with paired scDNA/scRNA-seq, systematically varying tumor purity, sequencing depth, and reference selection.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Numbat outperformed all others across most criteria; CopyKAT was the top choice when only an expression matrix was available and was uniquely robust at 1K UMIs per cell; SCEVAN achieved the best F1 for clonal breakpoint detection; Numbat showed high sensitivity for cnLOH but 14 of 17 LOH calls (~82%) were actually non-neutral CNV events; adding tumor microenvironment cells rescued inferCNV F1 scores for CRC02, CRC22, and CRC03 from 0, 0.5, 0.55 to 1.0, 0.9, 0.99 respectively.
abstract_sketch.impact: These findings offer practical guidance for tool selection and parameter tuning in single-cell CNA inference and identify open challenges for spatial transcriptomics and low-burden aneuploidy detection.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: scRNA-seq reveals transcriptomic heterogeneity in tumors and their microenvironment
CNAs play crucial roles in cancer initiation, progression, and therapy resistance
scDNA-seq is ideal but expensive and low-coverage
Expression-based tools (inferCNV, CopyKAT, SCEVAN) vs. expression+allele tools (Numbat, CaSpER)
No independent head-to-head benchmark with paired scDNA-seq ground truth exists
This study covers five tools, four tasks, and systematic purity/depth perturbations

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Benchmark datasets: CRC (n=8, HRA000201), glioma + NPC43 + HUVEC (GSE185269), neuroendocrine (PRJCA002946), ALL (n=2, GSE144296), gastric GX109
Tool versions: inferCNV 1.18.1, CopyKAT 1.1.0, SCEVAN 1.0.1, Numbat 1.3.2.1, CaSpER 0.2.0

Parameter choices: inferCNV tumor_subcluster_partition_method=random_trees; SCEVAN plotTree=FALSE; Numbat gamma=5 for Non-UMI, CRC22 min_LLR=1 max_entropy=0.6, CRC15 min_cells=1; CaSpER cnv.scale=loh.scale=3
Downsampling for purity: T:N ratios 1:100, 1:20, 1:4, 1:2, 2:1, 4:1, 20:1, 100:1, each resampled 50 times
Sequencing depth downsampling: ~10K, 3K, 1K UMIs/cell via samtools view -s with seeds 555, 666, 777
Subclonal hierarchical clustering: Euclidean + Ward.D2; optimal k by average silhouette
Genome heatmaps: 1Mb bins, weighted mean by intersection width

Statistics: paired Wilcoxon rank-sum (panel 2E), Kruskal-Wallis (panels 2C, 2H, 2J, 2L), unpaired two-tailed Wilcoxon + Benjamini-Hochberg (panel 3B); significance code NS: p>0.05, *: p<0.01, **: p<0.001, ***: p<0.0001
Visualization: R 4.1.2, ComplexHeatmap 2.10.0, ggpubr 0.4.0; pseudo-bulk CNV via DNAcopy 1.68.0 CBS + MergeLevels; LOH AB-allele via Sequenza
Breakpoint TP criterion: within 200kb of scDNA-seq breakpoint

subsections.title: Benchmarking framework and datasets
content_bullets: Five tools, two categories (expression-only vs expression+allele)
Paired scDNA/scRNA-seq as ground truth across CRC, glioma, neuroendocrine, ALL, cell lines
Four evaluation tasks: tumor-normal classification, CNA profile accuracy, subclonal inference, non-malignant aneuploidy

subsections.title: Distinguishing tumor from normal cells
content_bullets: Numbat performed best overall; CopyKAT best among expression-only tools
SCEVAN failed when tumor fraction <40% (CRC11, CRC12, CRC16, CRC15)
inferCNV was reliable except in tumor-dominated CRC02 (~88% tumor)
Including TME cells rescued inferCNV F1 in CRC02, CRC22, CRC03 from 0, 0.5, 0.55 to 1.0, 0.9, 0.99
In extreme low purity (CRC15 6% tumor), inferCNV performed well after TME inclusion
Numbat degraded sharply below 10K UMIs/cell; CopyKAT robust down to 1K UMIs/cell
SCEVAN's paradoxical F1 increase at low depth reflects filtering of non-malignant cells

subsections.title: Accuracy of inferred CNA profiles
content_bullets: Two-pass mode significantly improved inferCNV similarity in 8/9 cases
No single tool dominated CNA profile accuracy; Numbat and CaSpER output integer calls, others continuous
Tools performed well on solid tumors with sufficient tumor + normal cells (glioma, CRC13, CRC11, CRC12)
All four tools except Numbat performed poorly on cell lines alone
Breakpoint detection F1: SCEVAN best, inferCNV best precision, Numbat conservative; CopyKAT and SCEVAN produced thousands of segments/cell, Numbat/CaSpER tens to hundreds

LOH: 14/17 (~82%) of Numbat LOH calls were actually CNV amplifications or deletions; however Numbat correctly recovered all four cnLOH events in GX109 and identified chr5q, chr12q, chr17p cnLOHs in CRC13

subsections.title: Subclonal structure inference
content_bullets: Optimal k for CRC samples was typically 2 by silhouette analysis
Glioma C1 subclone: only Numbat correctly assigned as tumor (14/15 cells)
CRC03: only inferCNV failed; TME inclusion rescued it
CRC11: all tools achieved ARI > 0.8
CRC12: inferCNV and SCEVAN detected single-cell subclone

subsections.title: Aneuploidy detection in non-malignant cells
content_bullets: All tools performed poorly on fibroblast, T, B, endothelial aneuploidy
Non-malignant cells have lower UMI and gene counts than malignant
Non-malignant CNAs are typically whole-chromosome events with lower complexity

subsections.title: Computational performance
content_bullets: CopyKAT and SCEVAN fastest
inferCNV and CaSpER moderate
Numbat slowest; BAM-based allele counting dominates runtime
Laptop handles <1000 cells; larger datasets require HPC especially for Numbat and inferCNV

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Numbat best overall; CopyKAT best for expression-only and low-depth scenarios
Tumor purity drives inferCNV centering errors; TME inclusion mitigates this
Numbat LOH over-calls are genotype-modeling artifacts (1,0 vs 2,0 resolution)
Solid tumors easier than hematopoietic cancers or cell lines; less CNA complexity may explain
Non-malignant aneuploidy detection remains an unmet need
Depth matters differently across tools; Numbat most depth-sensitive
Parameter choices are sample- and task-specific (e.g., Numbat gamma)
Recommend pairing Numbat+SCEVAN/inferCNV when BAF is available, CopyKAT+SCEVAN/inferCNV otherwise
Limitations: scarce paired datasets, small cell counts, parameter sensitivity
Implications extend to spatial transcriptomics
